scope: Digital SAT Reading & Writing — answer choice engineering research (no DB writes)

web_sources: College Board Digital SAT Cognitive Lab Report & Technical Manual
Erica Meltzer / The Critical Reader (parallel structure, synthesis notes)
Test Innovators, Clear Choice Prep, AdmitStudio, PrepMaven distractor catalogues
methodology.db_query: Supabase MCP execute_sql on section='reading_writing' platform questions with 4 options

best_practices.summary: Official DSAT items use four-option MC with one best answer. College Board does not publish a universal same-length rule, but credible distractors must be grammatically parallel, semantically plausible, and free of length/tone giveaways. For Scholaris generation, length parity is a quality gate even when not an official CB requirement.

length_parity.target: Keep all four choices within ±20% character count and ±2 words of the median choice (stricter ±15% for grammar/punctuation items).
length_parity.flag_threshold: >30% spread between longest and shortest choice (current audit metric).
length_parity.exceptions: Boundaries/punctuation items may use single-token choices (comma, colon, em dash) — parity applies to token *class*, not sentence length. WIC synonym items may be 1–4 words each if all are single phrases.
anti_patterns: One full sentence correct + three word/phrase distractors (WIC, FSS)
Correct answer 2× longer than nearest distractor (SYN, COE quant)
Empty-string distractor for 'no punctuation' (BOU) — use visible token like 'no punctuation' instead
Placeholder distractors ('wrong', 'wrong2', 'correct meaning') in hard WIC
length_parity.myth_debunked: Longest answer is usually correct — FALSE per Erica Meltzer / evidence-based prep guides; length skew in our bank would teach a false heuristic.

rules: All four choices share the same syntactic frame: all full sentences, all infinitive phrases, all participial openers, or all punctuation tokens.
Matched opening patterns: e.g., 'It suggests…' / 'It argues…' / 'It claims…' / 'It rejects…' for main-idea items.
For COE quant: each choice cites comparable data density (two figures + comparison clause), not one long data sentence vs short rankings.
For SYN: each choice is one complex sentence accomplishing the stated goal; distractors omit one goal element, not shrink to half length.
For TRA hard: participial phrase + trailing clause on every option (already in longTraOptions).
For CTC/TSP (best in DB): outline-style sentences with parallel 'The passage…' frames — replicate this pattern elsewhere.
parallel_structure.grammar_specific: Two-part parallelism (Erica Meltzer): constructions on both sides of a conjunction must match ('noun + of + noun', 'adjective + because of + noun'). FSS/BOU distractors must swap only the tested feature.

near_miss_distractors: Half-right: uses passage vocabulary but misstates relationship or scope
Opposite-direction: flips causal/logical direction while sounding passage-native
Partial support (COE): true data comparison that does not test the full claim
Irrelevant trend (COE quant): accurate table fact that does not link variables in the claim
Extreme language: always/never/only/all — easy eliminations unless passage warrants
Surface-match (SYN): reuses note keywords but misses rhetorical goal element
Scope error (CID/INF): true for one paragraph, wrong for whole passage
oneprep_elimination_order: Too vague or too broad for question scope
hardness_techniques.difficulty_calibration: Hard items add subtle qualification errors in parallel-length choices, not shorter/wilder distractors. Challenged tier: two-sentence options (already validated) with matched sentence count per choice.

options_jsonb_schema.shape: Array of exactly 4 objects: { id: 'A'|'B'|'C'|'D', text: string, is_correct: boolean, explanation: string }
options_jsonb_schema.related_fields: correct_answer (text, must match is_correct option id); source_metadata.rw_subtype; skill_id

validateOptions: ids exactly A, B, C, D in order
every option has non-empty text and explanation
validateContentQuality: Passage sentence counts by tier/subtype (CTC, challenged stimulus, hard WIC passage)
Hard/challenged TRA: min word count per option, no simple transitions (However, Therefore, etc.)

existing_utilities.mkOpts: Builds 4-option array from parallel text/explanation arrays
existing_utilities.longTraOptions: Hard TRA generator with matched participial-phrase structure

validation_in_validateDsatRwQuestion.gap: No validateOptionLengthParity() — primary root cause of 75% spread failures

poetry_synthesis_bank.strengths: Handcrafted distractors with explicit near-miss types; poetry WIC uses 1-word parallel synonyms; TSP/CID use matched full-sentence frames
poetry_synthesis_bank.weaknesses: SYN correct answers often 2× longer than distractors; still 100% over 30% char-spread threshold in DB (12/12)
poetry_synthesis_bank.example_balanced: poetry-tsp-easy-001 — all four outline sentences ~70–95 chars
poetry_synthesis_bank.example_imbalanced: syn-hard-002 — correct ~170 chars vs distractors ~50–80 chars

coe_quant_bank.strengths: Data-dense parallel sentences; named distractor taxonomy (Data Misread / Partial Support / Irrelevant Trend); correct answer cites full claim
coe_quant_bank.weaknesses: Correct choices routinely longest (dual comparisons); distractors shorter single-metric statements — 9/9 over 30% spread
coe_quant_bank.example: coe-quant-easy-001 — A: 120 chars vs D: 68 chars (76% spread)

legacy_generators.dsat-challenged-bank.mjs: 15.4% over 30% (n=325) — best legacy generator due to 2-sentence option padding

sample_20_measurements.issue: correct answer cites dual-sector comparison; distractors single-metric
single-token choices — acceptable if all tokens, but D shortest
correct synthesis sentence 3× longer than distractors
simple transition words — medium tier allows short
correct answer phrase longer than single-word distractors
single-word WIC — spread from char length of words, not word count

worst_offenders.note: Top 25 by char_spread_pct; dominated by BOU hard items with empty-string distractors (min_chars=1, max_chars=16 → 1500% spread)

top_ids.pattern: long correct synthesis vs short distractors

rules: Compute char_spread_pct and word_spread_pct across A–D
Fail if char_spread_pct > 30 for passage-based subtypes (CID, INF, COE, SYN, TSP, CTC, WIC multi-word)
Fail if char_spread_pct > 30 for hard/challenged TRA/FSS unless grammar-token exception
Grammar-token exception: all choices <= 20 chars and same syntactic class (punctuation, pronoun, verb form)
BOU 'no punctuation' distractor must use visible label (e.g., 'no punctuation') not empty string

steps: 1. Write correct answer as one complete sentence (12–18 words).
2. Build three distractors using same opener + parallel clause structure.
3. Pad each distractor to median_char ± 15% by adding a qualifying phrase (not new claims).
4. Verify each distractor fails for one named trap (opposite, scope, unsupported).

COE_textual.template: Quoted excerpt of similar length (±15 words) per choice.
steps: 1. Select four passage spans of comparable word count.
2. Only one supports the claim; others are adjacent topic, wrong speaker, or wrong function.
3. Trim/pad quotations with ellipses consistently.
1. Express correct answer with full claim-aligned comparison (two data points).
2. Partial-support distractor: same length, true comparison missing one sector/variable.
3. Irrelevant-trend distractor: same length, accurate but non-diagnostic fact.
4. Data-misread distractor: same length, wrong pairing or threshold.
5. Pad with neutral context phrases to equalize length (see coe-quant-hard-003 rewrite target ~130 chars each).

SYN.template: Single complex sentence (~22–28 words) accomplishing the rhetorical goal.
steps: 1. Parse goal into 2–3 required elements.
2. Correct answer includes all elements in one sentence.
3. Each distractor omits exactly one element but matches word count via descriptive padding from notes.
4. No distractor shorter than 80% of correct length.

WIC.template_easy: Four single words or parallel phrases (2–4 words each) — same word count.
WIC.template_hard: Four synonymous phrases of 3–5 words each (not placeholders).
steps: 1. Replace 'wrong'/'wrong2'/'correct meaning' with real near-synonyms/antonyms.
2. Enforce equal word count across A–D.

TRA.template_hard: Participial opener (8–12 words) + main clause continuation on all four (use longTraOptions).

BOU_FSS.template: Matched punctuation tokens or matched verb/pronoun forms only.
steps: 1. Never use empty string — use 'no punctuation' or em-dash pair notation consistently.
2. All four choices same visual width class.

challenged.template: Two sentences per option (ensureTwoSentenceOption) with matched total length ±15%.

utility_to_add.name: balanceOptionLengths
utility_to_add.behavior: Pad distractors with neutral qualifiers drawn from CHALLENGED_OPTION_SUFFIXES pool until within parity band; never pad correct answer

priority_order: 1. BOU empty-string + hard punctuation outliers (patch-dsat-hard-bou.mjs pattern)

key_takeaways: 75% (3167/4225) of English RW questions exceed 30% choice-length spread — far above acceptable parity.
validateDsatRwQuestion has no length-parity gate; optionWordCount/charLen exist but are unused for spread.
CTC and TSP are the gold-standard patterns (0–0.6% failure); replicate their parallel 'The passage…' frames.
Even new handcrafted generators (poetry-synthesis, coe-quant) hit 100% spread flag — content quality improved but length engineering still needed.
dsat-challenged-bank (15.4% failure) shows 2-sentence option padding helps; extend to all subtypes.
